Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests failed because Kubernetes log streaming to linuxptp-daemon pods returned context deadline exceeded / timeout errors during the GetProfileLogID step, preventing the test from locating the ptp4l config log line needed to derive the foreign master clock ID.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Kubernetes pod log stream failure in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 tests — "Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=timedout waiting for matches


could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the detailed agent logs (prior identical run same day, pod linuxptp-daemon-5mcqg):

```

[ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-5mcqg container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: ... err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream ... err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Additionally, the API server was returning Unauthorized by 08:29 UTC (end of job), with the bc ClockSync tests running at 07:02–08:29 UTC — indicating progressive auth/credential degradation throughout the long 3.5-hour job:

```

error: You must be logged in to the server (Unauthorized)

E0624 08:29:47.118913 memcache.go:265] "Unhandled Error" err="couldn't get current server API group list: the server has asked for the client to provide credentials"

```

• Root Cause: The test helper GetProfileLogID in test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go opens a live Kubernetes log stream (Follow: true, no TailLines) via pods.GetPodLogsRegex searching for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[([^\]]*)\] within a 3-minute timeout. The stream — opened with context.TODO() so no test-side deadline — is being terminated by the infrastructure with context deadline exceeded before the regex can be matched. This error pattern repeats identically across all ClockSync tests in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha suites, confirming a cluster-wide log streaming connectivity issue rather than a daemon log format regression. The linuxptp-daemon does emit this log line (printNodeProfile in pkg/daemon/daemon.go:1369 writes the rendered Ptp4lConf with #profile: prefix and message_tag), but the HTTP connection carrying the pod log stream is dropped before the data reaches the test. The progressive Unauthorized failures at job end suggest the kubeconfig token was near expiry, which may also have disrupted API server proxy connections to node kubelets mid-run.
• Breaking PR/Commit:


TL;DR: T-GM GNSS lock loss (ClockClass 248/Freerun) collapsed the entire PTP sync chain, causing all 14 failures across all 5 suites.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/14 failures — T-GM GNSS unlocked (ClockClass 248), cascading to all BC/slave ClockSync tests (suites: bc ×3, dualfollower ×3, dualnicbc ×3, dualnicbcha ×2, tgm ×2): The WPC T-GM node in telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com lost GNSS lock and, critically, failed to enter holdover (ClockClass 7) — instead transitioning directly to Freerun (ClockClass 248). This is confirmed directly by the tgm suite ("expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248]", "Timed out waiting for clock class 7") and is the upstream cause of all ClockSync timeouts in every other suite: without a GNSS-locked grandmaster, ptp4l on BC/slave nodes never reaches SLAVE state and the GetForeignClockID log-stream helper never matches its regex, firing the 202-second timeout on every test. The same could not get any profile line / could not find regex in log stream error message appears across 6 different daemon pods (jvf75, 9ssfb, z7mfj, lkhnx, s8frf, cklkh, h4bbm) on multiple nodes.

• 1/14 failures — Prometheus not returning valid responses during CPU Utilization test (suite: bc ×1): The parallel CPU Utilization test in bc failed because Prometheus was returning no valid data. This is a secondary symptom of the same cluster-health degradation: a lab with a broken PTP grandmaster and, in some suites, API server credential expiry (Unauthorized at 07:53:36Z) is consistent with a broader cluster instability window on 2026-07-03.

• Contributing factor (not a separate root cause) — Kubernetes API server degradation mid-run: Some suites saw error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded (dualnicbcha ×2) rather than the log-stream timedout waiting for matches pattern (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc). The API server also emitted Unauthorized errors at job teardown. This is a compounding instability but does not change the root cause — the PTP tests would have failed regardless due to the absent grandmaster signal.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot causeSuite-specific?
bc3 ClockSync + 1 CPU-utilGNSS loss (ClockSync); Prometheus degraded (CPU-util)No — shared GNSS root
dualfollower3 ClockSyncGNSS lossNo
dualnicbc3 ClockSyncGNSS loss (most direct confirmation via tgm cross-reference)No
dualnicbcha2 ClockSyncGNSS loss + API server dropping streamsNo
tgm2 holdover/event testsGNSS loss / DPLL not entering holdoverPrimary source — not cascading from another suite

All 5 suites share a single upstream root cause. No failure is suite-specific. The tgm suite is the causal origin (DPLL hardware or GNSS disable behavior); all other suites are downstream victims.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Immediately: Verify and restore GNSS lock on the T-GM node. SSH to the cnfdf30/cnfdf31/cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com T-GM node, check linuxptp-daemon logs for GNSS lock status, and confirm the DPLL reports ClockClass 7 before re-triggering the nightly job. Check GNSS antenna connectivity and signal health (GPS signal availability at the lab site).

2. Investigate DPLL holdover behavior on GNSS loss. The tgm evidence shows the WPC NIC's DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED → FREERUN (ClockClass 248) without an intermediate HOLDOVER (ClockClass 7) period. This may be caused by the ubxtool CFG-NAVSPG-INFIL_NCNOTHRS=50 test-disabling mechanism producing an abrupt GNSS cutoff, or the hardware DPLL holdover timeout being shorter than the test's 3-minute polling window. Verify the holdover duration configuration on the WPC NIC and consider whether the test should wait longer or use a softer GNSS-disable method.

3. Investigate API server token TTL relative to job duration. The job runs for ~2h50m and the API server was issuing Unauthorized errors at teardown. If the CI kubeconfig token has a TTL shorter than the job duration, all long-running nightly jobs will degrade. Rotate or extend the credential lifetime in the telco5gran lab CI configuration.


Overall Issue Class: infra_other (hardware GNSS failure in the test lab, not a code regression or transient flake)


Overall Severity: HIGH — 14/14 failures in a nightly periodic job, all suites impacted, no code changes implicated. The job is completely blocked until GNSS lock is restored, but there is no OCP 4.20 code defect to fix.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (4 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync serial tests timed out unable to read the foreign-clock-ID from linuxptp-daemon-container pod logs, and the parallel CPU Utilization test failed because Prometheus was not returning valid responses — both symptoms of a systemic infrastructure failure on the test cluster on 2026-07-03.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: linuxptp-daemon log stream returns no foreign-clock-ID output (affects: "Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-jvf75 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded


could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-9ssfb container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded


could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for

openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-jvf75 container=linuxptp-daemon-container,

err=timedout waiting for matches

```

All three failures occur against different daemon pods (jvf75 for test-bc-master1/temp, 9ssfb for test-slave1), confirming the problem spans multiple nodes. The same could not find regex in log stream pattern with identical semantics appears in every other suite that ran on the same cluster (dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha) — 9+ failures total with the same root.

• Root Cause: The conformance tests open a Kubernetes log stream to the linuxptp-daemon-container and look for ptp4l lines containing the foreign-clock-ID (the grandmaster clock identity that ptp4l emits once it reaches SLAVE state, e.g. FOREIGN_MASTER_RECORD_SET or similar). When ptp4l is NOT synchronized — because the upstream PTP grandmaster is unavailable or the sync chain broke — those log lines are never produced and the test times out. The fact that every ClockSync test on every suite failed simultaneously against multiple different pods on multiple nodes points to a cluster-wide PTP environment failure (grandmaster or uplink down), not an isolated daemon crash. Configuration/API tests in the same suite (operator deployment, lease, namespace) all passed, confirming the daemon pods were running; only the PTP-sync-dependent tests failed.

The only candidate code change in the window is openshift/linuxptp-daemon commit b022e1d (OCPBUGS-95610, "Send ptp4l event when running without proxy", merged 2026-07-02, one day before the run). Inspection of the pre-OCPBUGS-95610 release-4.20 source (3c31e2f) shows that this commit adds tBCTransitionCheck only in the !stdoutToSocket branch (lines 1059-1082 of daemon.go) — the path taken when the cloud-event-proxy (CEP) sidecar is absent. The CI job deploys CEP (SIDECAR_IMG=...cloud-event-proxy:4.20), so stdoutToSocket=true and the !stdoutToSocket block is bypassed entirely. OCPBUGS-95610 is therefore not in the hot path for this job and is ruled out as the cause of these failures.

• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — no code change found on release-4.20 that would affect the stdoutToSocket=true (with-CEP) ptp4l sync path. Infrastructure instability on 2026-


TL;DR: Cluster log-stream collapse + API auth expiry wiped 22 of 24 tests; a linuxptp-daemon state-machine regression caused the remaining 2 TGM holdover failures.



Root Cause Groups:


• 12/24 failures — linuxptp-daemon log stream unreachable (ClockSync GetProfileLogID/GetClockIDForeign timeout): All ClockSync tests in bc (3), dualfollower (3), dualnicbc (3), dualnicbcha (2), and oc-serial test 1 (1) hit could not find regex in log stream … error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded or timedout waiting for matches. The test helper opens a live kubectl logs --follow stream and waits for a ptp4l profile or sync line that never arrives within 3–10 minutes. Two compounding factors: (a) ptp4l may not be reaching SLAVE state cluster-wide (no grandmaster reachable or log format changed by PR #613), and (b) when the API server began degrading, streaming connections were silently severed rather than cleanly refused.

• 10/24 failures — Kubernetes API server Unauthorized mid-run (oc-serial tests 2–11): After the first oc-serial test completed at ~07:29 UTC, the kubeconfig credentials expired or the API server rejected them (Unauthorized / couldn't get current server API group list: the server has asked for the client to provide credentials). Every subsequent test aborted immediately at the DaemonSet readiness check (linuxptp-daemon DaemonSet must exist before readiness wait → Unauthorized). One variant also showed dial tcp 10.8.34.117:6443: connection refused, indicating the API server briefly went fully unreachable.

• 2/24 failures — TGM holdover state-machine regression (tgm suite): Both T-GM holdover tests fail deterministically: disabling GNSS causes ts2phcState to be forced to PTP_FREERUN (not PTP_HOLDOVER) when NoSourceTSCount == 2 in linuxptp-daemon/pkg/event/event.go:402–403, making ClockClass 7 unreachable. Test 1 exhausted its 3-minute poll; test 2 observed map[metric:248] (freerun) throughout the 10-second sampling window. This is a deterministic regression, not a flake.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


PatternSuites affected
GetProfileLogID / GetClockIDForeign log stream timeoutbc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc (test 1)
API server Unauthorized cascadeoc (tests 2–11)
GNSS → ClockClass 7 holdover state-machine bugtgm

The log-stream failures are 100% cross-suite (5 of 6 suites, identical error text, different pod names) — confirming a cluster-wide cause rather than a suite-specific issue. The oc Unauthorized failures are oc-specific but are a secondary consequence of the same cluster degradation event. The tgm failures are suite-specific and code-driven.


No suite is entirely clean. dualnicbcha is the only suite where the log-stream failure count is below its total suite size (2 of 2 failed; no "passing" baseline in that suite either).



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Re-run the job immediately and monitor the PTP grandmaster. The log-stream and API-auth failures bear all hallmarks of a transient lab infrastructure event (grandmaster unreachable, credential rotation, API server instability on telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com around 2026-06-24 07:00–08:30 UTC). A clean re-run will confirm whether the 22 non-TGM failures are infra flakes.

2. Audit linuxptp-daemon PR #613 (0459bc0, "upstream-sync-2026-06-22") for ptp4l log-format changes. If the upstream sync altered the format of the Ptp4lConf: or selected best master clock log lines, the test regexes in ptphelper.go and pods.go will never match regardless of cluster health. Compare the ptp4l log output from a pre-PR-613 build against a post-PR-613 pod to confirm or rule out a log-format regression.

3. Fix the TGM holdover state machine in event.go:402–403. Remove or guard the unconditional ts2phcState = PTP_FREERUN override when NoSourceTSCount == 2; instead, allow the state to transition to PTP_HOLDOVER so the existing ClockClass 7 branch at lines 459–461 and 430–433 becomes reachable. This is a deterministic code regression and will not self-heal with a re-run.


Overall Issue Class: infra_other (dominant — 22/24 failures) with an embedded test_regression (2/24 tgm failures from PR #613 state-machine change)


Overall Severity: CRITICAL — the entire 24-test job failed; the cluster experienced mid-run auth collapse, and a code regression in the TGM holdover path is deterministic and will recur on every re-run.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 ClockSync tests failed because Kubernetes log streaming to linuxptp-daemon pods returned context deadline exceeded / timeout errors during the GetProfileLogID step, preventing the test from locating the ptp4l config log line needed to derive the foreign master clock ID.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: Kubernetes pod log stream failure in GetProfileLogID (affects: all 3 tests — "Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-hrsj8 container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=timedout waiting for matches


could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-fpgx7 container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

From the detailed agent logs (prior identical run same day, pod linuxptp-daemon-5mcqg):

```

[ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-5mcqg container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: ... err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream ... err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Additionally, the API server was returning Unauthorized by 08:29 UTC (end of job), with the bc ClockSync tests running at 07:02–08:29 UTC — indicating progressive auth/credential degradation throughout the long 3.5-hour job:

```

error: You must be logged in to the server (Unauthorized)

E0624 08:29:47.118913 memcache.go:265] "Unhandled Error" err="couldn't get current server API group list: the server has asked for the client to provide credentials"

```

• Root Cause: The test helper GetProfileLogID in test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go opens a live Kubernetes log stream (Follow: true, no TailLines) via pods.GetPodLogsRegex searching for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[([^\]]*)\] within a 3-minute timeout. The stream — opened with context.TODO() so no test-side deadline — is being terminated by the infrastructure with context deadline exceeded before the regex can be matched. This error pattern repeats identically across all ClockSync tests in bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, and dualnicbcha suites, confirming a cluster-wide log streaming connectivity issue rather than a daemon log format regression. The linuxptp-daemon does emit this log line (printNodeProfile in pkg/daemon/daemon.go:1369 writes the rendered Ptp4lConf with #profile: prefix and message_tag), but the HTTP connection carrying the pod log stream is dropped before the data reaches the test. The progressive Unauthorized failures at job end suggest the kubeconfig token was near expiry, which may also have disrupted API server proxy connections to node kubelets mid-run.
• Breaking PR/Commit:
2026-07-03T05:00
❓ infra
❌ failure4.214.21-e2e-telco5g-ptp311mtest_flake LOW
WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.
15 failed tests
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.

• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.

The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.

Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.

This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)



TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW

• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.

• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.

• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.
